Canada, West, Toronto District . . . . . The Staff Officer of Pensioners, Toronto, Canada, West, and the District Paymaster, Halifax, Nova Scotia.
Cape of Good Hope . . . . . The Staff Officer of Pensioners, Toronto.
Ceylon . . . . . The District Paymaster, Cape Town.
Cyprus . . . . . The District Paymaster.
Falkland Islands . . . . . Ditto.
Fiji Islands . . . . . The Colonial Treasurer.
Fort Garry . . . . . The Governor.
Gambia . . . . . The District Paymaster, Halifax, Nova Scotia.
Gibraltar . . . . . The Colonial Treasurer.
Gold Coast . . . . . The District Paymaster.
Honduras . . . . . The Acting District Paymaster, Cape Coast Castle.
Hong Kong . . . . . The Acting District Paymaster.
Jamaica . . . . . The District Paymaster.
Lagos (Africa) . . . . . Ditto.
Malta . . . . . The Colonial Treasurer.
Mauritius . . . . . The District Paymaster.
Natal . . . . . The Acting District Paymaster.
New South Wales . . . . . The District Paymaster, Pietermaritzberg.
New Zealand . . . . . The Colonial Treasurer, Sydney.
Nova Scotia (including London, Montreal, Ottawa, and part of Hamilton Districts) . . . . . The Colonial Treasurer, Wellington.
Queensland . . . . . The District Paymaster, Halifax.
Sierra Leone . . . . . The Colonial Treasurer, Brisbane.
South Australia . . . . . The Acting District Paymaster.
St. Helena . . . . . The Colonial Treasurer, Adelaide.

REGULATIONS FOR ADMISSION TO THE INDIAN STAFF CORPS.

(PUBLISHED WITH HORSE GUARDS G.O. 169 OF 1884.)

PART I.

Officers serving in India.

  1. 1. An officer serving in India will be eligible to become a Probationer on the following conditions:—
    1. I. He shall, at time of application, have completed one year's actual regimental duty whether in or out of India.
    2. II. He shall, at date of appointment to a Native regiment, have less than four years' service, and not exceed 25 years of age.
  2. 2. His application must be accompanied by certificates in the Forms A 1 or A 2 and D, and by a declaration, in duplicate, in the Form E.
  3. 3. An officer may be appointed to the Staff Corps of any Presidency, without reference to the Presidency in which his regiment may be serving.
  4. 4. The period of probation must be passed with Native troops, and the date of joining a Native Corps shall be the date of appointment on probation.
  5. 5. Should he not have passed the examination in the Native languages by the lower standard before joining a Native Corps, he will be required to do so within 18 months of so joining, otherwise he will cease to be a Probationer.
  6. 6. After the expiration of one year from the date of appointment on probation, the Probationer, provided he has passed the higher standard examination in the Native languages and the professional examination required under regulation, may be admitted to the Staff Corps.
  7. 7. On the expiration of 8 months from date of appointment on probation he will be admitted to the Staff Corps whether he has passed the above examinations or not, on production of certificates in the Forms C, D and E.
  8. 8. Should he not have passed the above examinations before admission to the Staff Corps, he will be allowed a further period of 18 months (or three years in all from date of appointment on probation) within which to pass.
  9. 9. Should he not pass within this period he will be removed from the Service

PART II.

Officers of Corps serving out of India.*

  1. 10. An officer of a corps serving out of India will be eligible to become a Probationer on the following conditions:
    1. I. He shall, at time of application, have completed one year's actual regimental duty; in the case of officers of West India regiments, two years.
    2. II. He shall, on the 1st of January of the trooping season during which he embarks for India, have less than four years' service, and not exceed 25 years of age.
  2. 11.† His application must be submitted through his commanding officer to the Military Secretary, Horse Guards, and be accompanied by certificates in the Forms B and D, and by a declaration in the Form E.
  3. 12.‡ An officer will not be allowed to volunteer for any particular Staff Corps, but on arrival in India he will be posted under the orders of the Government of that country to whichever of the three Corps the exigencies of the Service may demand.
  4. 13. If accepted, a candidate will be required to embark for India within two months of being so warned, and the date of embarkation for India shall be the date of his coming on the Indian Establishment.
  5. 14. The period of probation must be passed with Native troops, the candidate will be appointed to a Native Corps on first arrival in India, and the date of joining it will be the date of his appointment on probation.
  6. 15. Before the expiration of 18 months from date of joining a Native Corps, he must pass the examination in the Native languages by the lower standard, otherwise he will cease to be a Probationer.
  7. 16. After the expiration of one year from date of joining a Native Corps, the Probationer, provided he has passed the higher standard examination in the Native languages and the professional examination required under regulation, may be admitted to the Staff Corps.
  8. 17. On the expiration of 18 months from the date of joining a Native Corps, he will be admitted to the Staff Corps, whether he has passed the above examinations or not, on production of certificates in the Forms C, D and E.
  9. 18. Should he not have passed the above examinations before admission to the Staff Corps, he will be allowed a further period of 18 months (or three years in all from date of joining a Native Corps) within which to pass.
  10. 19. Should he not pass within this period, he will be removed from the Service.

* These Regulations are also applicable generally to officers of the Royal Marines.

† The application of an officer of Marines must be addressed to the Admiralty.

‡ A candidate may, however, express a preference for a particular Presidency. He should give the names of the three Presidencies in order of preference, and make known any special claims he may have for appointment to a particular one of them for the consideration of the military authorities in India.

PART III.General Regulations.

20. The names of Probationers will be retained on the lists of their British regiments until admission to the Staff Corps.

21. During the period of probation it will be open to the Commanding Officer of a Native Corps to represent to the Commander-in-Chief of the Presidency to which the Probationer belongs that he thinks the Officer, either from temper or other cause, is unsuited for employment with Native troops; in which case the Commander-in-Chief in India, if satisfied with the reasons, will post the Officer to his own regiment should a battalion of it be serving in India, or, should such not be the case, to any battalion in India pending orders from His Royal Highness the Field-Marshal Commanding-in-Chief.*

22. The same course will be followed in the case of an officer who may not, at the expiration of 18 months' duty, be able to obtain the necessary certificates for admission to the Staff Corps, and in the case also of an officer who has been permitted, under para. 23 to resign his probationary appointment.

23. No Officer will, however, be allowed to resign his probation except on sufficient grounds to be approved by the Commander-in-Chief in India, the Government of India, the Secretary of State for India in Council, and his Royal Highness the Field-Marshal Commanding in Chief. An officer applying for permission to resign will be required to produce a declaration and a certificate in the Forms F and G, but he will continue to serve with his Native regiment until his resignation is approved by the Secretary of State and His Royal Highness the Field-Marshal Commanding in Chief, his application being sent forward at once for the final orders of the Secretary of State for India.

24. A Probationer obtaining leave out of India either on private affairs, or for more than six months on medical certificate, will be held to have resigned his probationary appointment.

25. When an Officer is ultimately admitted to the Staff Corps the date assigned to his admission shall be that of his first appointment on probation.

26. An Officer shall not be eligible to hold any permanent or officiating appointment, regimental or other (except that of Squadron or Wing Officer), until he shall have passed the higher standard examination in the Native languages and the professional examination required under regulation.

27. No extension of the prescribed period within which these examinations must be passed will be allowed except on account of illness or delay resulting from employment on field service, and only then with the special sanction of the Secretary of State for India in Council.

28. An Officer removed from the Service under para. 9 or para. 19 will receive a free passage to Europe and a gratuity of one year's English pay of his rank.

29. Under special circumstances, or in special cases, the Secretary of State for India in Council may deviate from any of the above regulations.

NOTE.—These regulations must be understood as referring only to admission to the Staff Corps and to probation, and not to the conditions of Service in the Staff Corps.

CERTIFICATES AND DECLARATIONS.A 1.

Commanding Officer's Certificate.—For appointment as Probationer from a Regiment serving in India.

(Para. 2.)

Certified that Lieutenant _____ of the _____ regiment, a candidate for the _____ Staff Corps, has on this date completed _____ years' service from the date of his first commission, of which _____ years were (or the whole period of which was, as the case may be) spent in India.

Lieutenant _____ [†has passed an examination in the native languages by the higher (or lower) standard, and] has attained such a knowledge of his drill and duty as an officer of Infantry (or Cavalry, or Artillery battery) in all situations. He also, possesses a fair knowledge of the Army Act of 1881, of the Army (Annual) Act, and of the Queen's Regulations.

I further certify that during the period Lieutenant _____ has served with this regiment, he has been attentive to his duties, and that his conduct has been in all respects unexceptionable.

(Station and date.)

(Signature of the officer commanding the regiment.)

A 2.

Commanding Officers' Certificates.—Alternative which may be used for appointment as Probationer from a Regiment serving in India of an Officer of short service. (Para. 2.)

Certified that Lieutenant _____ of the _____ regiment, a candidate for the _____ Staff Corps, has on this date completed _____ years' service from the date of his first commission, of which _____ years were (or the whole period was, as the case may be), spent in India.

Lieutenant _____ [†has passed an examination in the Native languages by the Higher (or Lower) standard] has passed his drills, and has performed in a satisfactory manner his duties as an officer of Infantry (or

* An officer of Royal Marines ceasing to be a Probationer will not be attached to a British Regiment in India, but will at once be provided with passage to England, and will revert to the disposal of the Admiralty on arrival in that country.

† To be inserted if the Officer has so passed.

‡ Horse, field, or garrison, as the case may be.

STAFF CORPS REGULATIONS.

Cavalry, or * Artillery). He also possesses a fair knowledge of the Army Act of 1881, of the Army (Annual) Act, and of the Queen's Regulations.

I further certify that during the period Lieutenant has served with this regiment he has been attentive to his duty, and that his conduct has been in all respects unexceptionable.

(Station and date.)

(Signature of the officer commanding the regiment).

B.

Commanding Officer's Certificate.—For appointment as Probationer from a Regiment serving out of India.
(Para. 11.)

Certified that Lieutenant of the Regiment, a candidate for the Indian Staff Corps, has on this date completed years' service from the date of his first commissions. Lieutenant has attained such a knowledge of his drill and duty as an Officer of Infantry (or Cavalry, or Artillery) in all its branches as to qualify him for the command of a company (or troop, or battery) in all situations. He also possesses a fair knowledge of the Army Act of 1881, of the Army (Annual) Act and of the Queen's Regulations.

I further certify that during the period Lieutenant has served with this regiment, he has been attentive to his duty, and that his conduct has been in all respects unexceptionable.

(Station and date.)

(Signature of the officer commanding the regiment.)

C.

Commanding Officer's Certificate.—For admission to the Staff Corps. (Paras. 7 and 17.)

Certified that Lieutenant of the regiment, a candidate for the Staff Corps, has on this date completed years' service from the date of his first commission, of which years were (or the whole period of which was, as the case may be) spent in India.

Lieutenant has passed an examination in the native languages by the Higher (or Lower) standard, and has attained such a knowledge of his drill and duty as an officer of Infantry (or Cavalry) in all its branches as to qualify him for the command of a company (or troop) in all situations. He also possesses a fair knowledge of the Army Act of 1881, of the Army (Annual) Act, of the Native Articles of War, and of the Queen's Regulations, as well as of the Bengal (or Madras, or Bombay) Army Regulations, besides having gained some general acquaintance with the organization and nature of all branches of the Army serving in Bengal (or Madras, or Bombay).

I further certify that during the period Lieutenant has served with this regiment, he has been attentive to his duties, and that his conduct has been in all respects unexceptionable.

(Station and date.)

(Signature of the officer commanding the regiment.)

D.

Medical Officer's Certificate.—For appointment as Probationer and for admission. (Paras. 2, 7, 11, and 17.)

I certify that to the best of my belief Lieutenant is in good bodily health, and apparently well capable of undergoing the vicissitudes of service in India.

(Station and date.)

(Signature of the medical officer of the regiment.)

E.

Declaration of Candidate—For appointment as Probationer. (Paras. 2, 7, 11, and 17.)

I hereby declare, upon my honour as an Officer and a gentleman, that I have not, nor to the best of my belief has any person on my behalf or in my interest, directly or indirectly, received or agreed to receive any sum of money, valuable security, or other thing in respect of my appointment to the Indian Staff Corps; and I promise, honourably and unreservedly that I will not receive the same or in any manner recognize or benefit by its receipt hereafter.

(Signature of candidate.)

[The above declaration must be drawn up in the officer's own handwriting, and must be read over in the presence of the officer commanding the regiment, who will attach his certificate as witness to the declaration in the following manner:—]

Read over in my presence, and signed before me, this day of

18

(Signature of commanding officer.)

[The declaration must also be subscribed by a second witness, who may be either an officer of standing in the service, or a civilian, being a householder.]

F.Declaration of Candidate.—For resignation of probationary appointment. (Para. 23.)

I hereby solemnly declare, on my honour as an officer and a gentleman, that my resignation of my probationary appointment for the Staff Corps does not originate in any cause affecting my honour, or my character, or professional efficiency

(Station and date)

(Signature of probationer.)

G.Commanding Officer's Certificate.—For resignation of probationary appointment. (Para. 23.)

I hereby certify that the resignation by Lieutenant of his probationary appointment in the regiment under my command does not originate in any cause affecting his honour, or his fitness to continue in Her Majesty's service.

Station and date.)

(Signature of commanding officer.)

EXAMINATION IN THE NATIVE LANGUAGES BY THE LOWER STANDARD.

The following are the tests laid down for this examination:—

(a.) Reading fairly and construing with accuracy not less than half an octavo page of the undermentioned works:—

  1. 1. Bāgh-o-Bahār, the second darvesh, including the story of King A'zād Bakt.
  2. 2. Captain Jarrett's translation of Marshman's History of India, Hindu period. (Bengal and Bombay only.)

(b.) Conversing with an educated Native in the presence of the examiner on subjects likely to occur in the performance of regimental or professional duty, in the transaction of ordinary business, or in the course of every-day life. If this test is applied through the medium of a Native of India, he should speak plain Hindustani, free from peculiarities of idiom and dialect, and not a patois; and if the candidate proves his ability to comprehend readily all that is said to him and to make himself fairly intelligible, more should not be required.

A candidate who has passed the examination by the lower standard is entitled to a reward of Rs. 100.

EXAMINATION IN THE NATIVE LANGUAGES BY THE HIGHER STANDARD.

The following are the tests laid down for this examination:—

*(a.) Reading fluently and construing with readiness and accuracy not less than an ordinary octavo page of the undermentioned works:—

  1. (1.) Bāgh-o-Bahār.
  2. (2.) Selections from the prose of the Prem Sāzar.

*(b.) Translating accurately and with correctness of idiom and grammar, not less than half an ordinary octavo page of plain English into language similar to that of the Bāgh-o-Bahār in the Persian character, and an equal amount in the Hindi character, in language similar to that of the Prem Sāzar.

*(c.) Reading fairly and translating readily and correctly Hindustani manuscripts written in both the Persian and Hindi characters. These manuscripts may be selected from the proceedings of a case in court, from reports or petitions addressed to civil or military authorities, from letters passing between Natives of India in the ordinary course of business, or from private correspondence. They should not be written with the clearness of a printed book, nor yet in a very crabbed or cramped hand, but in such a manner as fairly and honestly to represent the written characters as practically employed in the Presidency or province in which the examination is held.

†(d.) Virda voce translation into the Oriental language of a paper of English sentences, and conversing with the examiners, or in their presence with persons selected by them, with fluency and such correctness of pronunciation, grammar and idiom, as to be at once intelligible.

A candidate who has passed the examination by the higher standard is entitled to a reward of Rs. 260 if he has previously passed by the lower standard, or of Rs. 360 if he has passed by the higher standard on first examination.

PROFESSIONAL EXAMINATION FOR ADMISSION TO THE STAFF CORPS.

The following are the tests for admission to the Staff Corps in virtue of qualifying appointments in Native regiments:—

(a.) The evolutions of a regiment of cavalry or a battalion of infantry (as the case may be) as laid down in the regulations of those services respectively, including skirmishing, duties of outposts, patrols, escorts, advanced and rear-guards.

(b.) The charge of a troop, company (as the case may be), or detachment in every position in which it may be placed, musketry instruction, requisitions, returns, and correspondence.

* Bengal and Bombay only. In Madras there are slight variations in the tests.

† Bengal only. The tests in Madras and Bombay vary slightly.

(c.) The Indian articles of war and the Army Regulations, India, particularly all such as affect the conduct of native troops, their pay, pension, clothing, &c.

(d.) The system of regimental interior economy pursued in the Native cavalry and infantry, and especially in the regiment to which the officer has been attached, including the mode of investigating and dealing with offences, complaints, and petitions from the men, method of keeping rosters for furlough and guard, deductions made from the men's pay and system of accounts, together with a knowledge of every article of equipment used by the men, their cost, and the mode of carrying them.

In the case of probationers for the Native cavalry the examination will include the additional subjects under head (c).

(e.) System of purchase of horses, and the districts whence they are usually obtained, their prevailing defects and good qualities, stable management, general knowledge of farriery, and the diseases and injuries to which horses are liable, with their treatment.

The committee will satisfy themselves by examination in the field and viva voce questions that the officer is instructed in the required subjects.

EXAMINATION FOR REGIMENTAL AND ARMY STAFF APPOINTMENTS.*

Officers of the Staff Corps are not qualified to hold the appointments of Commandant, Second in Command, or Wing Commander, nor to leave their regiments for the purpose of holding any appointment whatever (unless they come within the provisions of the following paragraphs), until they shall have passed an examination in the following subjects:—

PART I.

(a.)—Cavalry—

In the field:—

Command of a regiment in the following circumstances:—

Singly on parade.

As part of a brigade.

As advanced or rear guard.

As outpost covering a brigade or division.

Maneuvring on varied ground (adapting movements to the ground and to a supposed object)

(b.)—Infantry—

In the field:—

Riding

Command of a battalion in the following circumstances:—

Singly on parade.

As part of a brigade.

As advanced or rear guard.

As outpost covering a brigade or division.

Skirmishing (adapting movements to the ground and to a supposed object).

PART II.

(a.) Military law.

(b.) Tactics.

(c.) Field fortification.

(d.) Military topography.

Officers may be nominated at any time to staff appointments for distinguished service in the field, although they may not have passed the qualifying examination; but officers so appointed who are liable to examination under this order will be required to qualify within a reasonable time.

All officers in the Staff Corps who may have passed the final examination at the Staff College, will be exempted from the whole of the tests laid down.

PROMOTION.

Under Royal Warrant promotion in the Staff Corps is governed by such regulations as the Secretary of State for India may make from time to time.

The existing regulations are—

On permanent transfer to the Staff Corps Second Lieutenants are promoted to the rank of Lieutenant.

Officers after 11 years' service, of which four must have been in the Staff Corps, become Captains;

After 20 years' service, of which six must have been in the Staff Corps, they become Majors; and

After 26 years' service, of which eight must have been in the Staff Corps, they become Lieutenant-Colonels.

The periods of four, six, and eight years' service in the Staff Corps are exclusive of all leave of absence except privilege leave.

* The qualifications required and examinations to be passed for the judge advocate general's department, the administrative departments of the army, the survey department, and for civil and political employ, will be found in the Indian Army Regulations.

A Lieutenant-Colonel becomes eligible for promotion to the rank of Colonel in the Army after three years' satisfactory service with that rank. Selection for promotion will be made under such regulations as the Secretary of State in Council may lay down.

Officers above the rank of Lieutenant are also eligible for promotion by brevet as a reward for distinguished service in the field, or for distinguished service of an exceptional nature other than in the field.

Colonels rise to the ranks of Major-General, Lieutenant-General, and General on the fixed establishment of General Officers of the Indian Staff Corps, which is as follows:—

Generals ... ... ... ... 3
Lieutenant-Generals ... ... ... ... 8
Major-Generals ... ... ... ... 22

Officers in civil employ, after ten years' absence from military duty, are removed from the effective list of the army, and placed on a supernumerary list, rising thereon, under the regulations in force, to the rank of Lieutenant-Colonel, but to no higher rank.

This condition is, however, not applicable to officers of rank taken for high political or civil employment, or to officers taken for temporary civil or political duties in the field or in newly-acquired territories.

Daily.
s. d.

FAMILY PENSIONS.

The claims to pension of the widows and families of officers of the Staff Corps are treated under the provisions of such Royal Warrant regulating the grant of pension to the widows and families of officers of H. M.'s British Forces, as may be in force for the time being.

The following are the rates of ordinary pension:—

Pension to Widow. Compassionate Allowance to each Child.
Yearly. Yearly.
£ £
General Officer, including Brigadier-General 120 20
Lieutenant-Colonel or Brevet-Colonel 90 16
Major 70 14
Captain 50 12
Lieutenant 40 10

with increased pensions in the case of the widows and children of officers who have been killed in action or have died of wounds or illness contracted on field service.

Compassionate allowances to sons cease at 18 years of age, and to daughters at 21 years of age or marriage.

Widows and families are also entitled to pensions from the Indian Military Service Family Pension Fund, to which all officers must subscribe as a condition of their appointment to the Staff Corps.

The following are the rates of widows' pension:—

In Europe. In India.
Yearly.
£		 Yearly.
Rs.
Lieutenant-Colonel of five years' service in the rank (if subscribed for)† 160 1,609
Lieutenant-Colonel 130 1,356
Major 100 1,043
Captain 70 730
Lieutenant 40 417

Children's pensions (all ranks alike) dependent on age:—

In Europe from £10 to £45 per annum.

In India from Rs. 104 to Rs. 469 "

Pensions to sons cease at 21 years of age, to daughters on marriage.

ROYAL WARRANT FOR THE FORMATION OF THE UNEMPLOYED SUPERNUMERARY LIST FOR OFFICERS OF THE INDIAN ARMY.

VICTORIA, R. and I.,

By the Grace of God, of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland Queen, Defender of the Faith, Empress of India.

Whereas it has been represented to Us that it is expedient to provide for the transfer to an unemployed supernumerary list of General Officers and Colonels of Our Indian Forces who have attained certain limits of age, or who have been continuously unemployed for certain periods;

Our will and pleasure is that, with effect from and after the 1st day of July, 1881, the following rules shall be established for that purpose:—

  1. 1. Transfer to the retired list under Our Warrant of the 31st December, 1877, shall cease.
  2. 2. A Major-General shall be placed on the unemployed supernumerary list on attaining the age of 62 years, a Lieutenant-General or General on attaining the age of 67 years.
  3. 3. A General Officer shall be placed on the unemployed supernumerary list after being unemployed for five years continuously since his promotion to Major-General, or after being unemployed for seven years continuously from the time of his last employment as Colonel.

* This does not refer to the special rates of half-pay of unemployed General Officers who are effective officers.

† Subscription for this pension is voluntary.

4. A Colonel of the "local service," that is, an Officer whose appointment was not made subject to the conditions prescribed in the General Order of Our Governor General of India in Council, dated the 9th December, 1859, No. 1637, shall be placed on the unemployed supernumerary list, provided he is in receipt of Colonel's allowance and not in military employ.—

If he attained the rank of Lieutenant-Colonel before the 1st October, 1877,—at the age of 59 years until the 1st July, 1885, and after that date at the age of 58 years.

If he attained the rank of Lieutenant-Colonel on or after the 1st October, 1877,—at the age of 55 years.

*5. A Colonel or Lieutenant-Colonel of the "general list," that is, an Officer whose appointment was made subject to the above-mentioned General Order, shall be placed on the unemployed supernumerary list on attaining the age of 55 years.

6. In the event of an Officer being called to, or engaged on, active service in the field, at or about the time when, under any article of this Our Warrant, he would be liable to be transferred to the unemployed supernumerary list, Our Secretary of State for India in Council may, on the recommendation of Our Governor General in Council, extend his term of service during the continuance of the emergency as a supernumerary to the establishment.

7. If in any special case, not under the terms of the preceding article, it appears desirable to postpone the transfer of an Officer to the unemployed supernumerary list, Our Secretary of State for India in Council may, upon the recommendation of Our Governor General in Council, so postpone it.

8. Our Secretary of State for India in Council, upon the recommendation of Our Governor General in Council, may also bring back an Officer from the supernumerary unemployed list to the effective list, should it appear essential to the interests of Our Service to do so.

9. An Officer whose case has been specially treated under Articles 6, 7, or 8 shall, so long as he remains on the effective list, be supernumerary thereon.

Given at Our Court at Balmoral, this twenty-fourth day of October, in the year of our Lord one thousand eight hundred and eighty-one, and in the forty-fifth year of Our reign.

By Her Majesty's command.

(Signed) HARTINGTON.

ROYAL WARRANT FOR THE PROMOTION OF INDIAN OFFICERS SO FAR AS
RELATES TO RANK IN THE ARMY.

VICTORIA, R. and I.,

By the Grace of God, of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland Queen, Defender of the Faith
Empress of India.

Whereas it has been represented to Us that it is expedient to make further Regulations for governing the Promotion in the Army of the Officers of Our Indian Forces, Our will and pleasure is, that the following Regulations be established, and be the sole Regulations under which the Promotion in the Army of such Officers shall be effected, viz. :—

†1. A Captain may attain the rank of Major—

†(a) By regimental seniority ;

†(b) By twenty years' service in the Army in the case of Officers whose appointment to Our Indian Forces was made subject to the conditions prescribed in the General Order of Our Governor General of India in Council, dated the 9th of December 1859, No. 1637 ;

†(c) By brevet, in succession to a vacancy on the establishment of General Officers, provided such vacancy occurred before 1st October 1881 ;

†(d) By brevet, for distinguished service in the field ;

†(e) By brevet, for distinguished service of an exceptional nature other than in the field.

2. A Major may attain the rank of Lieutenant-Colonel—

†(a) By regimental seniority

†(b) By twenty-six years' service in the Army in the case of Officers whose appointment to Our Indian Forces was made subject to the conditions prescribed in the General Order of Our Governor-General of India in Council, dated the 9th of December 1859, No. 1637 ;

†(c) By brevet, after twenty-six years' service in the Army, so long as there remain any Majors on the list of Cavalry and Infantry in Our Indian Forces ;

†(d) By brevet, in succession to a vacancy on the establishment of General Officers, provided such vacancy occur before 1st October, 1881, or thereafter for so long as there remain any Majors on the list of Cavalry and Infantry who are not subject to the conditions prescribed in the General Order of Our Governor-General of India in Council, dated the 9th December 1859, No. 1637 ;

(e) By brevet, for distinguished service in the field ;

(f) By brevet, for distinguished service of an exceptional nature other than in the field.

*3. A Lieutenant-Colonel may attain the rank of Colonel—

*(a) By twelve years' service in the substantive grade of Lieutenant-Colonel in the "local service" ;

*(b) After thirty years' service in the Army, four of which shall have been in the grade of Lieutenant-Colonel ;

*(c) On being selected for the rank of Colonel under such regulations as our Secretary of State for India in Council may lay down; provided he shall have completed three years' satisfactory service as a Lieutenant-Colonel.

*(d) By brevet, on appointment to be one of Our Aides-de-Camp;

*(e) By brevet, as a reward for distinguished service in the field;

*(f) By brevet, for distinguished service of an exceptional nature other than in the field;

*(g) After four years' satisfactory service with the rank, whether substantive or brevet, of Lieutenant-Colonel—

(1) In any military appointment, except the command of a troop or company by an Officer with the substantive rank of Captain; and except also any appointment which our Secretary of State in Council may exclude from qualifying.

(2) In any civil situation approved by Our Secretary of State for India in Council upon the recommendation of Our Governor-General in Council and Commander-in-Chief in India, provided it be of a nature to afford practical experience likely to be afterwards of advantage in military service.

*(h) After five years' service, in any appointment, with the substantive rank of Lieutenant-Colonel.

*(i) After seven years' satisfactory service with the rank of Lieutenant-Colonel, as Equerry or extra Equerry to the Sovereign or the Prince of Wales.

Clauses (b), (g), (h), and (i) shall only apply to Officers who attained the rank of Lieutenant-Colonel before the 1st January 1887.

No period of acting staff service of less than six months' duration shall reckon towards the rank of Colonel unless it be converted into actual staff service by subsequent confirmation.

3a. If it shall be necessary to appoint an Officer to a situation which may be declared by Our Secretary of State for India in Council to be one which shall carry the rank of Colonel, although the Officer may not have the rank or service to qualify for promotion, the local and temporary rank of Colonel only shall, until he shall have completed the necessary qualifying service, be conferred on him while holding such appointment.

4. The rank of Brigadier-General shall be local or temporary only.

5. The names of General Officers shall be borne upon four separate lists. The first shall contain the names of those on the fixed establishment, and of those who having been promoted out of their regular turn shall be supernumerary to the fixed establishment; the second, those of Officers retired under Our Royal Warrant of the 31st December 1877; the third, those of Officers placed on the unemployed supernumerary list under Our Royal Warrant of the 24th October 1881; and the fourth, those who shall have retired from the service with the honorary rank of Major-General, Lieutenant-General, or General.

6. The names of the Colonels and General Officers of the Staff Corps now borne on the Indian List to regulate promotion shall remain on that list, and the names of the Officers of the Staff Corps henceforth promoted to the rank of Colonel shall be placed on that list, to be thereon retained in italics, until their death, retirement, or transfer to the unemployed supernumerary list, for the purpose of regulating the promotion of the Officers below them.

7. The fixed establishment of General Officers (including the names of Officers of the Staff Corps) shall be the following:—

Generals ... ... ... ... ... 4
Lieutenant-Generals ... ... ... ... ... 13
Major-Generals ... ... ... ... ... 38
Total .. 55

8. Every vacancy on the establishment of General Officers shall be filled by the promotion of the senior Colonel of the Indian Army; or by the moving up of the name of the senior Officer of the Staff Corps borne on the list to regulate promotion; or by the transfer of a General Officer from the supernumerary list as provided in Art. 14.

9. Every vacancy on the establishment in the rank of Lieutenant-General or in that of General shall be filled up in like manner by the promotion of the Senior Officer of the Indian Army in the grade next below, or by the moving up of the name of the Senior Officer of the Staff Corps borne on the list of that grade.

10. Temporary or local rank and command as Major-General, Lieutenant-General, or General may, for the convenience of Our Service, be conferred on an Officer of the next lower rank (whether he hold such rank permanently or temporarily), without regard to seniority.

11. A Colonel who shall vacate an appointment as temporary Major-General, may, upon the recommendation of Our Governor General in Council, be made an honorary Major-General.

12. Promotion may be conferred upon a Colonel, Major-General, or Lieutenant-General, without regard to vacancies in the establishment, for distinguished service in the field, and in such case the recommendation of Our Governor General in Council, detailing the services for which the Officer is promoted, shall be published in the General Orders of Our Indian Military Forces, and in the London Gazette.

13. Promotion may be conferred upon a Colonel, Major-General, or Lieutenant-General, without regard to vacancies in the establishment, for distinguished service of an exceptional nature other than in the field.

14. An Officer not below the rank of Colonel, promoted for distinguished service, shall be borne as supernumerary, and shall not be placed on the establishment in his new rank until the time when he would have attained such rank by seniority; that is to say, when the surviving Officer who stood next senior on the list to the Officer promoted under Art. 12 or 13, shall have attained the same rank on the establishment, the next vacancy shall be filled by the transfer of the supernumerary General Officer to the establishment.

15. A supernumerary General Officer promoted for distinguished service, and absorbed under Art. 14, shall take his seniority from the date of his promotion as published in the London Gazette.

16. Field Marshals shall be appointed at the will of the Sovereign, and the rank shall be conferred without reference to seniority. A General Officer, retired under Our Warrant of the 31st December 1877, or placed on the unemployed supernumerary list under Our Warrant of the 24th October 1881, shall remain eligible for promotion to the rank of Field Marshal.

17. A Major-General or a Lieutenant-General, retired under Our Warrant of the 31st December 1877, and a Colonel, a Major-General, or a Lieutenant-General placed on the unemployed supernumerary list under Our Warrant of the 24th October 1881, shall be promoted to higher rank when the Officer on the establishment who was next junior to him as a Colonel shall obtain such promotion.

*18. Every Officer permitted to retire on full pay not later than the 31st December 1887 may be granted a step of honorary rank on retirement.

Given at Our Court at Balmoral, this 10th day of November, in the year of our Lord one thousand eight hundred and eighty-one, and in the forty-fifth year of Our Reign.

By Her Majesty's Command.

(Signed) HARTINGTON.

ROYAL WARRANT FOR CONTINUOUS SERVICE OF ROYAL ENGINEER OFFICERS IN INDIA.

VICTORIA, R. & I.

Whereas it has been represented to Us that it is expedient to secure, for the purposes of the Government of India, the continuous service on the Indian Establishment of Officers of Our Corps of Royal Engineers.

Our Will and Pleasure is that the following Regulations be established for that purpose, subject nevertheless, to any modifications which may hereafter appear to us to be expedient.

Regulations.

1. Officers of Our Corps of Royal Engineers may, with the sanction of Our Secretary of State for India in Council, elect to render continuous service to the Government of India, and the election shall be open to (1) Officers who at the date of this Our Warrant are performing their first tour of service in India, or who may hereafter proceed to India for that purpose (the election to be made within four years of their first arrival in India or in cases where this period has already been completed on or before the 1st October 1886); (2) Officers who are now serving on the Indian Establishment, but who have served part of their time elsewhere than in India; (3) Officers who having previously served in India are now serving on the British Establishment; (the election in each of the latter classes to be made on or before the 1st October 1886).

2. Officers electing for such continuous service shall enjoy the conditions of Indian pay and allowances at present existing, subject to such modifications of the same as may at any time appear expedient.

3. They shall be eligible for leave under the conditions applicable to Officers admitted to Our Indian Staff Corps on or after the date of this Our Warrant.

4. They shall be subject to the same regulations as to military promotion, and liability to removal from the effective list, as apply to the other Officers of Our Royal Engineers.

5. They shall be required, should occasion arise, to serve elsewhere than on the Indian Establishment.

6. They shall be allowed, under conditions to be laid down by Our Secretary of State for India in Council, and with his sanction and that of Our Secretary of State for War, to exchange with Officers of the Corps serving on the British Establishment.

7. They shall be liable, in view to their becoming acquainted with the progress made in military science and engineering, and to their qualifying themselves for promotion in their Corps, to return to England to do duty at the School of Military Engineering, for such periods and under such conditions as may, from time to time, be prescribed by Our Secretary of State for India in Council in concurrence with Our Secretary of State for War, and such duty shall count as service for Indian pension.

8. The Regulations under which, with the view of securing the flow of promotion in Our Corps of Royal Engineers, Officers of the Corps may be placed on half pay or retired, shall not be held to disqualify the Officers on the Indian Establishment so affected from continuing to hold any appointments in the Civil Departments of the Government of India which they may have been holding previously, and, subject to the discretion of the Government of India, their tenure of such appointments shall be regulated by the rules which apply to other persons holding similar appointments in the same Departments.

9. Officers permitted to elect for continuous Indian service shall be eligible, except as is hereinafter laid down, for pensions under the conditions and at the rates applicable to Officers admitted to Our Staff Corps after the 1st July, 1881.

10. Service for pension shall be reckoned from date of first commission, and it shall include all service on the effective list, and on the half pay or retired list provided for in Article 8 of this Our Warrant.

11. An Officer serving on the Indian Establishment at the date of this Our Warrant, who has served part of his time elsewhere than in India, or an Officer serving on the British Establishment at this same date, who may hereafter proceed to India, shall on retirement receive a pension consisting of the retired pay to which he would be entitled under Our Warrant regulating the retired pay of Our Royal Engineers generally, and, in addition, such a portion of the difference between the said retired pay and the Staff Corps pension belonging to his length of Indian service as is represented by the proportion his Indian service bears to his total service. In calculating the period of Indian service for pension, three years of British service shall be reckoned as Indian service.

12. Should an Officer retire before he has served a sufficient time to entitle him to a pension under the rules applicable to the Staff Corps as aforesaid, he shall be granted only such retired pay or gratuity as he would have been entitled to receive under Our Warrant regulating the retired pay of Officers of Our Royal Engineers generally had he not elected for continuous Indian service.

13. Officers who have already had periods of leave under conditions which allowed them to count such periods as service for pension shall, in the event of their volunteering for permanent service in India, be allowed the benefit of counting such periods towards pension under the conditions of this Our Warrant.

14. Officers of the permanent Indian Establishment transferred to the half pay list on account of ill health shall come under the same conditions as Officers of the Indian Staff Corps.

15. Our Secretary of State for India in Council shall be the sole administrator and interpreter of this Our Warrant, and shall be empowered to issue such instructions in reference thereto as he may from time to time deem necessary.

Given at Our Court at Windsor, this twentieth day of February 1886, and in the forty-ninth year of Our Reign.

By Her Majesty's Command,
KIMBERLEY.

REGULATIONS FOR THE EXAMINATION OF CANDIDATES FOR THE APPOINTMENT OF SURGEON IN HER MAJESTY'S INDIAN MEDICAL SERVICE.

1. The regulations are those in force at the present time. They are subject to any alteration that may be determined on.

Limits of age. 2. All natural-born subjects of Her Majesty, between 21 and 28 years of age at the date of the examination, and of sound bodily health, may be candidates. They may be married or unmarried. They must possess a diploma in surgery or a license to practise it, as well as a degree in medicine, or a license to practise it in Great Britain or Ireland.

Declaration to be submitted 3. They must subscribe and send in to the Military Secretary, India Office, Westminster, so as to reach that address at least a fortnight before the date fixed for the Examination, a declaration according to the annexed form.

4. This declaration must be accompanied by the following documents:—

(a) Proof of age either by extract from the register of the parish in which the candidate was born, or where such extract is unattainable, by his own declaration (pursuant to the Act 5 and 6 Will. 4, c. 62), form of which can be obtained at the India Office. A certificate of baptism which does not afford proof of age will be useless.

(b) A recommendation from some person of standing in society—not a member of his own family—to the effect that he is of regular and steady habits, and likely in every respect to prove creditable to the service if admitted; and a certificate of moral character from a magistrate, or a minister of the religious denomination to which the candidate belongs.

(c) A certificate of registration, in accordance with the Medical Act of 1858, of the degrees, diplomas, and licenses possessed by the candidate.

Physical Examination. 5. The physical fitness of each candidate will be determined previous to examination by a Board of Medical Officers, who are required to certify that his vision is sufficiently good to enable him to perform any surgical operation without the aid of glasses. A moderate degree of myopia will not be considered a disqualification, provided it does not necessitate the use of glasses during the performance of operations, and that no organic disease of the eyes exists. Every candidate must also be free from all other organic disease, and from constitutional weakness, or other disability likely to unfit him for military service in India.

Subjects for Examination 6. On producing the foregoing qualifications, the candidate will be examined by the Examining Board in the following compulsory subjects, and the highest number of marks attainable will be distributed as follows:—

Marks.
a. Anatomy and Physiology .. .. . 1,000
b. Surgery .. .. . 1,000
c. Medicine, including Therapeutics, the diseases of women and children .. .. . 1,000
d. Chemistry and Pharmacy, and a practical knowledge of Drugs .. .. . 1,000

N.B.—The examination in medicine and surgery will be in part practical, and will include operations on the dead body, the application of surgical apparatus, and the examination of medical and surgical patients at the bedside. The examination in chemistry will be limited to the elements of the science, and to its application to medicine, pharmacy, and practical hygiene.

No candidate shall be considered eligible who shall not have obtained at least one-third of the marks obtainable in each of the above compulsory subjects.

Voluntary subjects. 7. Candidates may be examined in the following voluntary subjects, for which the maximum number of marks obtainable will be:—

Marks.
French, German, and Hindustani (150 each) .. .. . 450
Natural Sciences .. .. . 300

The natural sciences will include comparative anatomy, zoology, natural philosophy, physical geography, and botany with special reference to materia medica.

A number less than one third of the marks obtainable in any of these voluntary subjects will not be allowed to count in favour of a candidate who has qualified in the compulsory subjects.

The knowledge of modern languages being considered of great importance, all intending competitors are urged to qualify in French and German.

8. The appointments announced for competition will be filled up from the list of qualified candidates arranged in order of merit, as finally determined by the total number of marks each has obtained in both the compulsory and voluntary subjects.

The Examiners in London will prepare a list in order of merit, with the marks affixed in the different subjects, to be transmitted to the Director-General and communicated to the

Professors of the Army Medical School. If any Candidate is found to be deficient in any particular subject, this shall be stated, in order that he may receive special instruction on the point at Netley.

9. After passing this examination, the successful candidates will be required to attend one entire course of practical instruction at the Army Medical School as Surgeons on probation in— Course at Netley.

  • (1) Hygiène.
  • (2) Clinical and Military Medicine.
  • (3) Clinical and Military Surgery.
  • (4) Pathology of diseases and injuries incident to Military Service.

These courses are to be of not less than four months' duration; but candidates who have already gone through a course at Netley as candidates for the Army or Navy Medical Service may, if thought desirable, be exempted from attending the school a second time.

10. During the period of residence at the Army Medical School, each candidate will receive an allowance of 8s. per diem, with quarters, or when quarters are not provided, with the usual lodging and fuel and light allowances of a subaltern, to cover all costs of maintenance; and he will be required to provide himself with uniform (viz., the regulation undress uniform of a Surgeon of the British service, but without the sword). Pay while at Netley.

11. All candidates will be required to conform to such rules of discipline as the Senate may from time to time, enact. Uniform while at Netley.

12. At the conclusion of the course, candidates will be required to pass an examination on the subjects taught in the School. The examination will be conducted by the Professors of the School. Examination at Netley.

The Director-General, or any Medical Officer deputed by him, may be present and take part in the examination. If the candidate gives satisfactory evidence of being qualified for the practical duties of an Army Medical Officer, he will be eligible for a commission as Surgeon.

13. The position of the candidates on the list of Surgeons will be determined by the combined results of the preliminary and of the final examinations, and, so far as the requirements of the service will permit, they will have the choice of Presidency in India, according to their position in that list. Position on List of Surgeons, how determined.

N.B.—There will be an examination for 4 appointments on 20 August 1888 and following days

DECLARATION AND SCHEDULE OF QUALIFICATIONS, TO BE FILLED UP BY CANDIDATE.

Christian and Surname at full length.

I, _____, years of age in _____ last, vide accompanying certificate, a Candidate for employment as Surgeon in Her Majesty's Indian Medical Service, do hereby attest my readiness to engage for that service, and to proceed on duty immediately on being gazetted.

I declare that I labour under no mental nor constitutional disease, nor any imperfection or disability that can interfere with the most efficient discharge of the duties of a Medical Officer.

(The Dates of Graduations and the Universities or Colleges are to be stated.)

  • I have the Degree of A.M. or A.B. from the _____
  • I have the Degree of M.D. or M.B. from the _____
  • I have a License to Practise Medicine from the _____
  • I have a Diploma in Surgery from the _____
  • I have a License to Practise Surgery from the _____

The Candidate should here state whether he has served in the Medical Department of the Army or Navy, or in the Subordinate Medical Department in India.

(Signature at full length)

(Date)

(Place of Residence)

Candidates who desire to undergo the voluntary examination are to sign the following declaration:—

It is my intention to undergo the voluntary examination in *

N.B. This paper when filled up is to be returned, under cover, to the Military Secretary, India Office, London, S.W

MEMORANDUM REGARDING THE POSITION OF MEDICAL OFFICERS TO BE APPOINTED TO HER MAJESTY'S INDIAN FORCES.

INDIA OFFICE, June, 1887.

1. This memorandum is based on the regulations in force at the present time. They are subject to any alteration that may be determined on.

PASSAGE TO INDIA.

2. Surgeons on appointment will, if possible, be provided with passage to India by troopship; but when troopship accommodation is not available, passage at the public expense will be provided by steamer, or a passage allowance will be granted, if preferred. A charge for messing during the voyage will be made at the rate of 2s. a day. This payment will not include the cost of liquors, which will be charged for as extras.

3. All Surgeons who shall neglect or refuse to proceed to India under the orders of the Secretary of State for India within two months from the date of leaving Netley, will be considered as having forfeited their appointments, unless special circumstances shall justify a departure from this regulation.

* State what subjects will be taken up.

INDIAN MEDICAL SERVICE.PAY PREVIOUS TO ARRIVAL IN INDIA.

4. The rate of pay drawn by Surgeons from the date of passing the final examination at the Army Medical School until arrival in India will be 10s. a day. (For rates subsequent to their landing in India, see paras. 16, 17, and 18.)

Pay at the above rate will be issued in this country up to the date of embarkation, and an advance of two months' pay at the same rate will also be made prior to embarkation, which will be adjusted in India in accordance with the rate laid down in para. 14.

GRADES AND PRECEDENCE.

5. The grades of Medical Officers in the Indian Military Forces are six in number, viz.:-

Royal War-
rant, 30th
April, 1887.
Arts. 1 and 2.

  1. (1.) Surgeon-General.
  2. (2.) Deputy Surgeon-General.
  3. (3.) Brigade Surgeon.
  4. (4.) Surgeon-Major.
  5. (5.) Surgeon.
  6. (6.) Surgeon on probation.

6. For purposes of precedence and other advantages attaching to the corresponding military rank, officers in the above grades rank as follows:-

As Major-General.
Surgeons-General,

As Colonel.

Deputy Surgeons-General.

As Lieutenant-Colonel.

Brigade Surgeons, ranking among themselves according to their commissions as such.
Surgeons-Major, after 20 years' service, or under 20 years' service, if specially advanced (see para. 11) for distinguished service in the field; junior to all Brigade Surgeons.

As Major.

Surgeons-Major under 20 years' service, unless advanced to rank as Lieutenant-Colonel for distinguished service in the field (see para. 11).

As Captain.

Surgeons.

As Lieutenant.

Surgeons on probation.

PROMOTION.

7. A Surgeon is promoted to Surgeon-Major on completion of twelve years' Service from date of first Commission.

Royal War-
rant, 30th
April, 1887.
Arts. 4 and 5.
Royal War-
rant, 30th
April, 1887,
Arts. 6 and 7.
Royal War-
rant, 30th
April, 1887,
Art. 8.
Royal War-
rant, 30th
April, 1887,
Art. 9.

8. In cases, however, of emergency, or when the good of the service renders such alteration desirable, it is competent for the Secretary of State for India, on the recommendation of the Viceroy and Governor-General of India in Council, to shorten the period of service above mentioned, in such manner as he shall deem fit and expedient.

9. All promotions from the rank of Surgeon-Major to that of Brigade Surgeon, from the rank of Brigade Surgeon to that of Deputy Surgeon-General, and from the rank of Deputy Surgeon-General to that of Surgeon-General, are given by selection for ability and merit.

10. On appointment as Honorary Physician or Honorary Surgeon to Her Majesty an Officer below the rank of Deputy Surgeon-General shall be promoted to that rank.

11. For distinguished service in the field an Officer of the Indian Medical Service may be promoted from any rank to that next above it, remaining supernumary in the higher rank until he would have been promoted in ordinary course, or a Surgeon-Major ranking as Major may be advanced to rank as Lieutenant-Colonel.

12. Time passed on half-pay will in no case be reckoned as service in calculating claims for promotion.

TENURE OF OFFICE IN ADMINISTRATIVE GRADES.

13. The tenure of office of Surgeons-General and Deputy Surgeons-General is limited to five years.

14. Deputy Surgeons-General, if not disqualified by age, are eligible either for employment for a second tour of duty in the same grade, or for employment in the higher grade of Surgeon-General by promotion thereto.

15. Absence on leave in excess of six months on medical certificate, or of four months on private affairs, involves forfeiture of appointment.

PAY AND ALLOWANCES.

16. The following are the rates of "Indian pay and allowances" which will be drawn by Officers hereafter appointed to the Indian Medical Service:-

Rank. Years' Service. Indian Pay and Allowances per mensem.
When not holding an appointment carrying higher pay.
R. A. P. R. A. P.
Brigade Surgeon and Surgeon-Major... 25 and over 888 12 0
" ... .. 20 " 852 3 7
" ... .. 15 " 677 6 11
" ... .. 12 " 640 14 6
A. B.
When not receiving any Staff Allowance. When receiving Staff Allowance in addition. (See para. 18, and note on next p.)
R. A. P. R. A. P.
Surgeon ... .. 10 " 451 14 5 410 9 5
" ... .. 6 " 433 10 2 392 5 2
" ... .. 5 " 355 12 2 304 14 2
" ... .. under 5 317 8 0 286 10 0

Despatch to Govt. of India, No. 112, of 14th April, 1881.
Royal War-
rant, 30th
April, 1887.
Despatches to the Government of India, Nos. 340, dated 7th November, 1864, para. 31, and 150, dated 15th June, 1876.
Despatch to the Government of India, No. 340, dated 7th November, 1864, para. 29.
India Army Circs., 6th Feb. and 20th May, 1885.

17. On first appointment they will only come into receipt of "Indian pay and allowances" (Column A) from the date of their arrival in India.

Despatch to Govt. of India, No. 265, of 26th Sept., 1878.
Despatches to Govt. of India, No. 340, of 7th Nov., 1864, paras. 28 & 30, & No. 167, of 23rd Aug. 1866, para. 12. Order of Govt. of India (Home Dept.) No. 150, dated 15th March, 1880.

18. Officers holding the principal administrative appointments and substantive military charges of the Indian Medical service receive at present the following consolidated salaries:—

Rs. per Mensem.
Surgeon-General, Bengal ... 2,700
" Madras ... 2,500
" Bombay ... 2,500
Deputy Surgeon-Gen. From 2,250 to 1,800
Brigade Surgeon or Surgeon-Major of 20 years' service and upwards in substantive charge* of a Native regiment 1,000 with Rs. 90, horse allowance in Cavalry regiments.
Brigade Surgeon or Surgeon-Major under 20 years' service in substantive charge of ditto 800 with Rs. 90 ditto
Surgeon above 5 years' full pay service in substantive charge of a Native regiment 600 with Rs 60 ditto
Surgeon under 5 years' ditto in substantive charge of a Native regiment 450 with Rs. 60 ditto

19. The salaries of other substantive medical appointments in the Civil and Military Departments are consolidated, and vary from Rs. 1,800 to Rs. 400 per mensem.

Despatch to Govt. of India, (Financial), No. 29, 27th Jan., 1878.

20. Qualified officers of the Medical Service are also eligible for appointments in the Assay Department. The salaries of these appointments are from Rs. 600 to Rs. 2,250 per mensem.

Despatch to Govt. of India, No. 235, 16th Nov., 1866, para. 7.

21. Medical Officers are required to perform two years' regimental duty in India before they can be considered eligible for civil employment.

22. No Medical Officer, however employed, can receive any staff allowance in addition to the pay laid down in para. 16 unless he has passed the examination in Hindustani known as the "Lower Standard." The passing of this examination does not of itself bring any increase of pay to an Officer, unless appointed to a substantive or officiating charge; but failure to pass disqualifies an Officer, even when holding such substantive or officiating charge, from receiving any portion of the staff allowances of the appointment.

23. Surgeons-General and Deputy Surgeons-General on vacating office at the expiration of the five years' tour of duty, will be permitted to draw in India respectively an unemployed salary of Rs. 1,200 per mensem in the former, and Rs. 900 in the latter case, for a period of six months from the date of their vacating office, after which they will be placed while unemployed on the following scale of pay per diem:—

Despatch to Govt. of India, No. 310, dated 7th Nov., 1864, para. 33.

Surgeon-General. Deputy Surgeon-General
£ s. d. £ s. d.
After 30 years service on full pay 2 5 0 1 14 0
" 25 " " " 2 5 0 1 10 0
" 20 " " " or on promotion should this period of service not be completed 2 0 0 1 8 0

LEAVE RULES.

24. Officers of the Indian Medical Service, below the rank of Deputy Surgeon-General, may be granted:—

Despatch to India, No. 92, dated 15th April 1886.

  • (1.) Privilege leave under such regulations as may from time to time be in force.
  • (2.) Leave out of India, for no longer period than one year, capable of extension to two years' absence from duty, on the following pay:—
After arrival in India, on first appointment £200 a year.
After the commencement of the 5th year's service for pension... 250 "
Ditto, ditto, 10th ditto 300 "
Ditto, ditto, 15th ditto 400 "
Ditto, ditto, 20th ditto 450 "
Ditto, ditto, 25th ditto 500 "
  • (3.) Leave in India, but for the period of one year only, on full military pay and half the Staff salary of appointment.

25. No extension of leave involving absence from duty for more than two years, whether taken in or out of India, can be granted except on specially urgent grounds and without pay.

26. An officer, unable on account of the state of his health to return to duty within the maximum period of two years' absence will, unless he is under para. 25 specially granted an extension of leave without pay, be placed on temporary half pay or the retired list, as the circumstances of the case may require. An officer will also be liable to be placed on half pay or the retired list should his health require an undue amount of leave, whether in or out of India.

27. Leave may be granted at any time, but solely at the discretion of the Commanders-in-Chief, or of the Local Governments in India in the case of officers not under the direct orders of a Commander-in-Chief.

28. An officer on leave, whether in India or out of India, will be required to rejoin at once on being recalled to duty, unless certified by a Medical Board as unfit to do so.

Despatch to India, No. 150, of 15th June 1876.

29. Officers of the Administrative Grades are entitled during their tenure of appointment to six months' leave of absence on sick certificate, or four months' leave on private affairs.

* See note on next page.

† Officers in Civil Employment, while they continue in such employment, are not affected by these rules.

HONOURS AND REWARDS.

30. Officers of the Indian Medical Service are eligible for the military distinction of the Order of the Bath, and for good service pensions.

Statutes of the Order of the Bath, and Despatch to the Government of India, No. 24, of 25th Jan., 1866.

Royal Warrant, 30th April 1887 Art. 12.

Despatch to Government of India, No. 112, dated 14th April, 1881.

Despatch to Government of India, No. 340, dated 7th November, 1864, paras. 36 to 38

Despatch to Govt. of India, No. 166, dated 24th June, 1869, para. 17.

Royal Warrant, 30th April, 1887, Art. 10.

Royal Warrant, dated 16th Nov. 1880, Art. 11.

Despatch to Government of India, No. 112, dated 14th April, 1881.

RETIRING PENSIONS AND HALF-PAY.

32. Officers of the Indian Medical Service will be allowed to retire on the following scale of pension, on completion of the required periods of service:—

£
After 30 years' service for pension ... .. 700
" 25 " " " " " " " " " " " " 500
" 20 " " " " " " " " " " " " 365
" 17 " " " " " " " " " " " " 292

33. Time of service for pension will reckon from date of arrival in India, and will include all leave taken under the rules quoted in paras. 24 to 29. The period of residence at the Army Medical School will also reckon as service for the above pension, except in the cases of Officers who have gone through the course at Netley as candidates for the British Army or Navy Medical Service.

34. A Surgeon-General, after five years' active employment in India in that grade, will be entitled to retire upon a pension of £350 per annum, in addition to that which he may be entitled under the above scale.

35. A Deputy Surgeon-General will, after five years' active employment in India in that grade, be entitled to retire upon a pension of £250 per annum, in addition to the pension to which he may be entitled under the above scale.

36. In each of the above cases stated in paras. 34 and 35, six months' absence on medical certificate will be allowed to count towards actual service in those grades. (See para. 29).

37. Four extra pensions of £100 a year each are annually allotted to the senior officers of the Indian Medical Service, in the proportion of two to the Bengal, and one each to the Madras and Bombay Services. They are offered in rotation to Surgeons-General, Deputy Surgeons-General, and Brigade-Surgeons; but officers of the two highest grades are only eligible for these extra pensions when, under certain conditions, they are unable to complete their full tour of service.

38. A Surgeon-General or Deputy Surgeon-General who has completed his term of service and has reverted to British pay, may reside in Europe, at the same time qualifying for higher pension.

39. With a view to maintain the efficiency of the service, all Medical Officers of the rank of Brigade Surgeon and Surgeon-Major shall be placed on the Retired List when they shall have attained the age of fifty-five years, and all Surgeons-General and Deputy Surgeons-General when they shall have attained the age of sixty years. In any special case, where it would appear to be for the good of the service that the Officer should continue in employment, he may be so continued, subject in each case to the sanction of the Secretary of State for India in Council.

40. Medical Officers not entitled to pension, when placed on temporary or permanent half-pay under para. 26, will be granted the British rate of half-pay of the corresponding military rank as under:—

Rank. Corresponding Military Rank. Rates of Half-pay.
Per Diem. Per Annum.
s. d. £ s. d.
Brigade Surgeon Lieut.-Colonel ... .. 11 0 200 15 0
Surgeon-Major of 20 years' service ... .. 9 6 173 7 6
Surgeon-Major ... .. Major ... .. 7 0 127 15 0
Surgeon ... .. Captain ... ..

Officers cannot retire in India on half-pay (No. 54, 8th February 1865).

41. The period passed on half-pay will in no case be reckoned as service in calculating claims for promotion or to pension on retirement.

WOUND PENSIONS.

42. Medical Officers are entitled to the same allowances on account of wounds and injuries received in action as are granted to combatant Officers of Her Majesty's Indian Military Forces holding the corresponding military rank.

Note referred to on previous page: Substantive and Officiating Charges:—

Medical charges are of two kinds:—substantive and officiating. The first is held by an Officer as his fixed and permanent appointment. For this he is entitled to the monthly consolidated pay laid down in the above scale (para. 18), subject to the condition specified in para. 22.

An officiating charge is held by an Officer who is acting temporarily in an appointment during the absence of the Officer whose substantive charge it is. The Officer thus officiating receives not the consolidated pay of the appointment laid down in the scale (para. 18), but the Indian pay and allowances (para. 16) of his own grade, plus half the staff allowance of the appointment in which he is officiating.

An Officer's staff allowance is the difference between his "Indian pay and allowances" (para. 16) and "consolidated pay" (para. 18) as given in the scales. The staff pay of an appointment is the difference between the Indian pay and allowances and the consolidated pay of the permanent incumbent. It has been ruled, however, that the extra allowance for an officiating charge shall never be less than Rs. 100 per mensem.

Example 1. A Surgeon of less than 5 years' service officiates for one in charge of a regiment of the same period of service. He receives Rs. 286. 10 + Rs. 100 = Rs. 386. 10.

Example 2. A Surgeon of less than 5 years' service, officiating for a Surgeon of 6 years' service in charge of a regiment, receives Rs. 286. 10 + \frac{\text{Rs. } 600 - \text{Rs. } 392. 5. 2}{2} = \text{Rs. } 390. 7. 5.

Royal Warrant, 30th April, 1887, Art. 2.

MILITARY FURLOUGH REGULATIONS.

lxiii

FAMILY PENSIONS.

43. The claims to pension of widows and families of Medical Officers shall be treated under the provisions of such Royal Warrant regulating the grant of pensions to the widows and families of British Officers as may be in force at the time being. (The Royal Warrant of 10th June 1854 is the one now in force.)

44. The widows and families of Medical Officers are also entitled to pensions from the Indian Military Service Family Pension Fund, to which all Surgeons must as a condition of their appointment, subscribe from the date of their arrival in India.

Despatch to the Government of India, No. 132, of the 30th June, 1866. Family Pension Regulations.

MILITARY FURLOUGH REGULATIONS OF 1854.

With the view of adapting the regulations to the present increased facilities of communication, and to correct, as far as possible, any tendency in them to deter officers from resorting to the places which may be most conducive to the restoration of their health, when a change of climate is required for that purpose, the following rules are established, and are to have effect from the 1st February, 1854:—

1. All regimental officers in the service on the 1st February, 1854, who prefer retaining what may be considered the advantages of the old regulations, may do so, provided they signify their wish to that effect when they next apply for furlough or leave of absence, without any limitation whether such leave be in India or elsewhere; and officers who once make their election will not be entitled to any other choice, but will be afterwards subject to the new or old regulations, according to their decisions.

2. Staff officers, as such, have not the above option allowed them, but they may retain the advantages of the old regulations by giving up their appointments, which, in such cases, will be considered vacated from the date of embarkation.

3. To those officers who adopt them, the new regulations will apply retrospectively in respect of furlough and leave of absence beyond sea, and in computing their time of service for retirement, so that their furloughs or leaves of absence, whether under the old or the new rules, and whether on private affairs or sick certificate, within or beyond India, will count for service to the authorized extent only, in computing the time for retirement on full pay.

4. Officers who have availed themselves of the option of the old rules are allowed to reconsider the question under the modifications now sanctioned.

5. Officers who have not taken leave subsequent to the publication of the new rules on the 1st February, 1854, and who, consequently, may not have been called upon to declare whether they would abide by the old or the new regulations, will, on retirement from the service, be entitled to take full advantage of the privileges given by the new regulations.

FURLOUGHS ON PRIVATE AFFAIRS.

6. Every officer will be allowed a furlough to Europe, or elsewhere beyond sea, for a period of two years at the expiration of ten years' service, and for a second period of two years after twenty years' service in India.

7. The second furlough of two years will only be granted after ten years' service in India, from the date of the return to duty after the first furlough of two years. These furloughs are not to be cumulative, under any circumstances, but are limited to two years. Officers who have taken leave of absence on sick certificate to Europe, or elsewhere beyond sea, will be ineligible to a furlough on private affairs until they have served six years from their last return to duty. This rule does not apply to short leaves to sea.

Officers who take their first furlough after twenty years' service in India, may take their second furlough after an additional service of five years from the date of their first return to duty. (G.O. 16 June, 1856.)

8. All grants of furlough on private affairs are subject to the exigencies of the public service, and to the regulations framed for and preservation of the efficiency of each regiment by limiting the number of absentees.

9. Officers holding staff or other detached appointments, may be allowed furlough for a period of six months without losing their appointments; but, during that period, they will not be entitled to any portion of their staff salary, which will be drawn in full by the officers officiating for them.

Absence for six months under this rule does not preclude an officer from taking his furlough to Europe after ten years' service. (G.O. 16 June, 1856.)

10. All officers, whether staff or regimental, while on furlough on private affairs, will be entitled to "Furlough Pay" only.

LEAVE OF ABSENCE ON SICK CERTIFICATE.

11. Leave of absence on sick certificate will be granted for a term not exceeding eighteen months, whether it be to any place in Europe, or elsewhere out of India.

12. Extensions of such leave may be granted, as at present, on production of medical certificates, for further periods not exceeding eighteen months in the whole.

13. An officer holding a staff or other detached appointment, will be allowed to be absent on sick certificate in England, or elsewhere beyond sea, for a period of twenty months, without losing his appointment; and, during the first six months only of such absence, he will be entitled to draw half the staff salary of his appointment, the officer officiating for him drawing the remaining half salary during the first six months, and the whole salary during the remainder of the time.

14. An officer's claim to half staff salary for six months, as sanctioned in the preceding rule, will not be affected by his subsequently retiring from the service in Europe or elsewhere.

15. No portion of command allowance, medical staff salary, or other similar allowance, which under the old rules was inadmissible to officers absent from their corps or stations, at the Cape or elsewhere, will be allowed to absent officers under these regulations.

16. All officers, whether staff or regimental, while on leave of absence on sick certificate, will be entitled for a period of six months to the same rate of Indian pay and allowances as was allowed under the old regulations to officers proceeding to the Cape on medical certificate, and for the remaining period of their leave they will receive furlough pay only.

17. If the leave on sick certificate be extended, furlough pay will be continued; but under no circumstances for a longer period than three years altogether, from the commencement of the absence.

STAFF OFFICERS.

18. The term, "staff officers," applies to all officers holding staff or detached appointments, military (general or regimental), political, or civil; also to general officers commanding divisions, members of the Medical Board, superintending surgeons, and others who, under the old rules, were entitled to draw half staff allowance when absent at the Cape.

19. Staff officers absent on leave beyond the periods stated in Rules 9 and 13 respectively, cannot retain their appointments and allowances except on special grounds to be sanctioned by the Government of the presidency. Any such special grant of leave is to be reported at once, with the reasons for it, and to be subject to approbation.

20. The privilege of retaining staff appointments during leave of absence on sick certificate, or on furlough on private affairs will not be available a second time, if the fresh leave is on sick certificate, until an officer has been three years, or, if on private affairs, until he has been six years in the discharge of the duties of his appointment, from the date of last return to it.

21. This rule, however, does not apply to leaves of absence, whether on sick certificate or on private affairs in India, or for brief periods to Ceylon, the Mauritius, or other places out of India.

22. Officers commanding troops of Horse Artillery and Light Field Batteries, and officers generally in employment, will be allowed leave out of India under the same rules with respect to allowance and tenure of command or office, as regulated the grant of leave to the Cape to such officers under the old regulations, except as to the duration of those advantages, the time for their

continuation being subject to the provisions of the new regulations.

DATE OF FURLOUGH AND ADVANCES OF PAY.

23. The period of furlough on private affairs, or leave of absence on sick certificate, is to be calculated from the date of the departure from India of the ship in which the officer proceeds.

24. Advances of pay and allowances, -staff and regimental, to officers proceeding on leave of absence on sick certificate, who may be entitled to them, will be made in India for three months from date of embarkation. Payments in continuation will be made in England at the rates of exchange fixed annually for repayment of advances made on account of Her Majesty's Government. Furlough pay will continue to be issued in advance for six months when required by officers obtaining furlough on private affairs.

25. The existing regulations regarding passage-money to officers are not affected by these rules.

26.
